selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No single-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this exact Angular prompt; the available Angular basics coverage is broader than the question-specific comparison needed here.

competitors.label: Angular Component Lifecycle Guide
competitors.selectionReason: The official lifecycle guide is the canonical source for when Angular creates, checks, and destroys component instances.
competitors.winReason: The lifecycle guide is authoritative, but our page is more useful for this specific ngIf question because it ties teardown to form resets, recreated providers, and a direct ngIf-versus-hidden toggle example.
theirStrengths: It authoritatively explains initialization, check, render, and destroy hooks for Angular components.
theirGaps: It is not centered on the very specific ngIf mental model of subtree teardown versus simple hiding.

realWorldUseCases:
Debug mental model

*ngIf does not hide a subtree; it creates and destroys an embedded view. In production, that difference shows up as reset form state, fresh child instances, repeated init work, and cleanup bugs when subscriptions or listeners are tied to that subtree.
The lifecycle guide explains destruction and ngOnDestroy clearly, but our page is more direct about the real ngIf bugs teams hit.
actionableExamples: The lifecycle guide explains hook timing well, but our page now gives the more direct destroy-versus-hide toggle example with preserved versus reset state.
followUpConfusion: It explains lifecycle ordering well, but it is less explicitly framed around ngIf versus hide/show decisions.
Keep one short line on how repeated teardown affects child cleanup and re-init work when the branch flips often.

competitors.label: Angular University ngIf Complete Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This is a strong tutorial competitor because it focuses directly on ngIf and the real template patterns teams use around it.
competitors.winReason: Angular University stays broader on ngIf usage, but our page now wins the lifecycle-reset question with a sharper destroy-versus-hide example and clearer follow-up around recreated subscriptions.
theirStrengths: It goes deep on ngIf usage patterns and common structural-template patterns.
theirGaps: It is broader on template composition and less tightly centered on the lifecycle-reset interview answer.

followUpConfusion:
Worked toggle scenario

If a child component keeps local draft text, a pending timer, or a component-scoped service, flipping *ngIf false tears all of that down. Flipping it true creates a fresh child, reruns init hooks, and starts new subscriptions or async-pipe subscriptions inside that subtree. Use hiding when the requirement is "keep the current instance and its local state alive"; use *ngIf when the requirement is real teardown.

realWorldUseCases: Angular University keeps the page tightly focused on ngIf in real templates, so the use-case framing stays close.
actionableExamples: Angular University has more breadth, but our page now gives the tighter destroy-versus-hide worked example that targets the exact lifecycle reset bug interviewers ask about.
followUpConfusion: Our page is now more explicit about recreated async-pipe subscriptions, fresh child instances, and the hide-versus-destroy decision boundary.

realWorldUseCases: Keep the answer focused on teardown, state reset, and hide-versus-destroy instead of drifting into generic control-flow syntax.

competitors.label: Swiftorial Template Syntax
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a direct competitor because it covers *ngIf inside template syntax and contrasts structural versus attribute directives at a basics level.
competitors.winReason: Swiftorial explains ngIf in context, but our page now gives the stronger toggle example and the clearer destroy-versus-hide follow-up that this question is really testing.
theirStrengths: It covers *ngIf alongside other template syntax and shows the directive in context.
theirGaps: It does not go far into state reset, component recreation, or lifecycle cleanup when the branch flips.

followUpConfusion:
Summary

*ngIf creates/destroys an embedded view. True = build DOM + instantiate components/directives and run init hooks. False = remove DOM + destroy instances and run ngOnDestroy. Use it when you want real teardown and to skip change detection for that subtree; use hiding when you need to keep state alive.

realWorldUseCases: Swiftorial explains *ngIf as a structural directive, so the practical framing is close.
actionableExamples: Its examples are clear, but our page now gives the more actionable toggle example that makes state reset and instance reuse visible immediately.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the likely next confusion: *ngIf destroys and recreates, while hiding keeps state alive.

realWorldUseCases: Keep one example where local form or child component state is visibly lost after a toggle.
